coredata.dc:title: Preparation of panchromatic carbon dots by drug function preservation strategy and its intracellular application for cancer diagnosis and therapeutics
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Jiahui
Ning, Juan
Hu, Guizhen
Guo, Wenzheng
Zhou, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: Using hydroxyurea and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, a series of panchromatic carbon dots were synthesized by varying the kind and amounts of solvents, and the pink carbon dots (p-CDs) with a quantum yield of 46% were chosen for cellular research. After further modifying the p-CDs with mannose, mp-CDs could selectively target liver cancer cells that overexpressed the mannose receptor and easily enter the cells via endocytosis to achieve naked-eye discrimination of liver cancer cells. In addition, the residual hydroxyurea functional group on the surface of mp-CDs is in coordination with mannose, which induces targeted killing effects on liver cancer cells. This work not only suggests a strategy for making panchromatic carbon dots by altering the solvent, but it also offers a way to identify and treat liver cancer through cellular research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Panchromatic
Hydroxyurea
Mannose
Target
Therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible emission and formation mechanism of full-color CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (a), XRD spectra (b), Raman spectra (c), and photoluminescence decay curves (d) of b-CDs, g-CDs, y-CDs, p-CDs, and r-CDs.
High-resolution XPS C 1s (a, b, c), N 1s (d, e, f), and O 1s (g, h, i) spectra of b-CDs, p-CDs, and r-CDs.
(a) Hydrated particle size of p-CDs and mp-CDs. (b) Zeta potential of p-CDs and mp-CDs. FT-IR spectra (c), and XRD spectra (d) of p-CDs, mp-CDs, and mannose.

Confocal images of Hela cells (a), RAW264.5 cells (b), HL-7702 cells (c), and HepG2 cells (d) incubated with mp-CDs for 30 min. (e) Relative mean fluorescence intensities in the green and red channels were measured by Image J software. Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). The scale bar was 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal images of Hela cells (a), RAW264.5 cells (b), HL-7702 cells (c), and HepG2 cells (d) incubated with p-CDs for 30 min. (e) The relative average fluorescence intensity of green and red channels in HepG2 cells was measured by Image J software, with the fluorescence intensity of mp-CDs entering the green and red channels as the reference value. Data were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). The scale bar was 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Flow cytometry analysis of the relationship between Annexin V-APC (a) and 7-AAD (b) channels and the number of dead cells in HepG2 cells incubated with p-CDs (blue) and mp-CDs (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Synthetic pathway of panchromatic carbon dots, and illustration of the synthetic pathway of mp-CDs and targeting and potentially treating cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Preparation of panchromatic carbon dots by drug function preservation strategy and its intracellular application for cancer diagnosis and therapeutics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using hydroxyurea and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, a series of panchromatic carbon dots were synthesized by varying the kind and amounts of solvents, and the pink carbon dots (p-CDs) with a quantum yield of 46% were chosen for cellular research. After further modifying the p-CDs with mannose, mp-CDs could selectively target liver cancer cells that overexpressed the mannose receptor and easily enter the cells via endocytosis to achieve naked-eye discrimination of liver cancer cells. In addition, the residual hydroxyurea functional group on the surface of mp-CDs is in coordination with mannose, which induces targeted killing effects on liver cancer cells. This work not only suggests a strategy for making panchromatic carbon dots by altering the solvent, but it also offers a way to identify and treat liver cancer through cellular research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the key challenges in cancer research is to identify cancer cells using chemicals on vectors as targets and killing cancer cells [1–3]. The synthesis of the commonly-used carriers that target cancer cells, such as mesoporous silica [4] and polymeric nanoparticles [5,6], is tedious and time-consuming. Due to its ease of synthesis, superior water solubility, and low toxicity, carbon dots, a nanomaterial made of carbon with great luminous performance, are frequently employed in environmental monitoring, biosensing, and drug delivery [7–15]. Studies have sought to explore longer wavelengths of fluorescent probes for improved visualization in vivo imaging of cells and animals since short-wavelength carbon dots are more susceptible to background interference and have little penetrating capabilities in practical applications [16–22]. The luminescence properties of carbon dots can be used in biological imaging, and partial carbon dots also have the function of cancer treatment.

Some of the active functional groups of the precursors are retained during the carbonization of carbon dots by solvent thermal dehydration. Wang et al. used two Chinese herbs as precursors to synthesize carbon dots with improved blood–brain barrier permeability after cranial injury [23]. Xing and Liu et al. used gentamicin and levofloxacin to synthesize antibacterial fluorescent CDs, respectively [24,25]. Hydroxyurea is an anticancer drug that mainly inhibits the production of ribonucleotide reductase, which is the only enzyme in the body that can catalyze the reduction of ribonucleotide to the corresponding deoxyribonucleotide, and at the same time can hinder the synthesis and repair of DNA [26–30]. Compared with carbon dots synthesized with organic small molecules or precursors of biomass [31–36], the preparation of carbon dots using drugs as precursors not only has good water solubility, low toxicity, and excellent optical properties, but also can play a role in the treatment of tumor cells. In order to facilitate pharmaceutical therapy for cancer, the prerequisite is achieving rapid targeting of the corresponding cancer cells through appropriate biomarkers. The mannose receptor is an important cancer biomarker that is abnormally upregulated in liver cancer cells and presents only presents in trace amounts in normal cells, making it possible to differentiate cancer cells by detecting its expression level [37–39]. As the ideal ligand for mannose, mannose has high affinity and stability in the microenvironment of cells [40,41]. Related studies have shown that mannose and its conjugates are different from other nanoparticles in that they readily enter cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. Meanwhile, mannose can greatly enhance the anti-cancer action of the drug. Its chemotherapy effect of the anti-cancer drug alone was significantly less than that of mannose combined with doxorubicin, and the adverse impact of mannose on liver cancer cells was minor [39,42]. Therefore, the synthesis of carbon dots and modification of mannose with the anticancer-drug hydroxyurea as a precursor has the potential to achieve recognition and induce apoptosis of liver cancer cells.

In this paper, the solvent thermal approach was used to produce a variety of panchromatic carbon dots using hydroxyurea and o-phenylenediamine. The method can retain the active groups modified on the precursors. By varying the type and ratio of solvents, blue, green, yellow, pink, and red carbon dots with a luminescence range of 455–675 nm were synthesized (Scheme 1 ), and their luminescence mechanisms were specifically examined using cyclic voltammetry for theoretical calculations and FT-IR, XRD, and XPS for various characterization techniques. The substrate-modified mannose was chosen using the p-CDs with the highest fluorescence quantum yield among the panchromatic carbon dots. mp-CDs were able to rapid and selective target HepG2 cells by the overexpressed mannose receptors on the surface of liver cancer cells, resulting in a strong fluorescence signal. After entering the cells via endocytosis, the active functional groups retained by hydroxyurea amplify the therapeutic effect on cancer cells with the synergy of mannose. The use of hydroxyurea as the precursor not only prepared full-color carbon dots but also provided a method to identify and treat liver cancer cells in cellular experiments by employing the hydroxyurea function retention strategy.

0.1 g of hydroxyurea and 0.03 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF aqueous solution (vol:vol = 1:1), and the mixed solution was ultrasonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the obtained CDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and an excess mixed solution of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (vol: vol = 3:1) was added to the solution to wash away the residual organic molecules. Finally, the solution was centrifuged with 8000 r min−1 for 10 min, and the supernatant was concentrated by rotary evaporation at 50 °C, and then p-CDs were obtained in a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C overnight. 0.05 g of mannose was dissolved in 10 mL of p-CDs solution, sonicated for 10 min to completely dissolve, and kept in a water bath at 25 °C for 10 h to obtain mp-CDs. Synthesis of other carbon dots was shown in supporting materials.

We used quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 55 %) and Rhodamine B (dissolved in ethanol, QY = 56 %) as references, and calculated the QY of the carbon dots using the following equation.
φ =
φ R
I
I R
A R
A
where
φ
is the fluorescence quantum yield, subscript R is the standard reference fluorophore, I is the measured fluorescence emission intensity, and A is the absorbance value.

First, the exposed glassy carbon electrodes were polished in an orderly manner with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm alumina powder. Then, to obtain a perfect electrode surface, the bare glassy carbon electrode was sonicated in water and ethanol for 5 min, respectively. 8 μL of carbon dots solution was added dropwise to the electrode surface and dried naturally overnight. Cyclic voltammetric curves of prepared carbon dots were examined in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). The potential scanning range covered from −1.6 to 0.4 V, and the scan rate was 0.3 V/s.

The apoptosis induced by carbon dots was detected by flow cytometry with Annexin V-APC/7-AAD apoptosis detection kit. After incubation of HepG2 cells with p-CDs and mp-CDs at 50 μg/mL for 24 h, cells were then digested and collected by centrifugation. Then resuspend in 100 µL binding buffer, add 5 µL Annexin V-APC (single staining), 10 µL 7-AAD (single staining) and 5 µL Annexin V-APC, 10 µL 7-AAD (double staining), respectively, incubate the cells for 15 min at room temperature. Finally, flow cytometry was used to analyze the apoptosis of different cell samples.

In this study, a variety of characterization methods were used to conduct specific analyses of the colored carbon dots. In Fig. S1, it can be seen in transmission electron microscopy that the p-CDs are well dispersed without obvious aggregation and have an average diameter of approximately 11.2 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the five samples in Fig. S2 display the peak at 280 nm observed in the UV region of the five samples corresponding to the π-π* transition of CN in the carbon nucleus[43]. The presence of absorption peaks between 300 and 420 nm in the low energy region of the samples is caused by different surface states and heteroatom doping, which is usually caused by n-π* transitions of CN or CO [19,44,45]. The unique absorption peaks of r-CDs at 575 and 600 nm may be related to the absorption of CO [11,46].

The fluorescence spectra are demonstrated in Fig. S2, our CDs exhibit excitation-independent properties except for p-CDs, and the emission maxima of the four samples locate at 460, 510, 570, and 675 nm, respectively. Notably, the fluorescence emission of p-CDs gradually changed in the range of 585–610 nm. The introduction of different functional groups on the surface of carbon dots is the main factor leading to the existence of multiple emission sites/defect sites[19]. The normalized fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S3) displays that the emission peak of CDs gradually redshifted, covering the entire visible spectrum. Meanwhile, the corresponding Commission International de L’Eclairage coordinates of the spectra (Fig. S2f) could visualize the color-tunable fluorescence more directly. Through the above spectrogram, it could be found that the panchromatic carbon dots are synthesized.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1 a display that the CDs have abundant functional groups, such as OH/NH at 3500–3100 cm−1, CO at 1750 cm−1, and CO at 1040 cm−1, which ensure have good solubility[46]. In b-CDs, g-CDs, and y-CDs the vibrational bonds of OH/NH in 3500–3100 cm−1 are broad and complete, but the vibrational bands in p-CDs and r-CDs are more discrete. In general, a wider OH vibrational band indicates that there are multiple hydroxyl structures on the surface of the material[47], resulting in higher polarity and hydrophilicity. In the b-CDs, g-CDs, and y-CDs, there is almost no absorption peak of carboxylic CO at 1750 cm−1. However, in p-CDs and r-CDs, there are sharp absorption bands, indicating the formation of carboxyl groups on their surfaces.

The XRD pattern in Fig. 1b reveals a broad peak near 25.5°, which is attributed to the typical vibration of graphitic carbon [46]. For Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1c), the D-band (1350 cm−1) and G-band (1580 cm−1) correspond to sp3 and sp2 carbon. The intensity ratio (IG/ID) is often used to indicate the degree of graphitization, and the ratio from b-CDs to r-CDs were 0.990, 0.997, 1.004, 1.012, and 1.045, respectively, indicating the increased size of the sp2 domains [48]. Comparing their intensity ratios, it can be found that with the change of solvent, the number of defects in the CDs increases, and the degree of order decreases [19,49]. In addition, as displayed in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence decay lifetime from b-CDs to r-CDs decayed from 6.46 ns to 1.53 ns, accompanied by a red-shift in fluorescence emission. Such short and descending lifetimes imply that the recombination pathways are dominated by the core states, and a larger sp2 domain leads to the faster decay rate [17,46]. The decay of fluorescence decay life can be fitted by the double exponential formula, which involves a short-life component τ1 (about 1 ns) and a long-life component τ2 (about 10 ns), which are assigned to the recombination processes in the core states and the surface states [46]. From b-CDs to r-CDs, as the average lifetime decays from 6.46 ns to 1.53 ns, the proportion of τ1 increases from 18.84 % to 83.92 %, indicating that the role of the core state in these radiative lifetimes increases with the red shift of luminescence.

Three samples of b-CDs, p-CDs, and r-CDs were used for XPS analysis to investigate the degree of surface oxidation and graphitization. Three typical peaks are shown in Fig. S4: C 1 s (290 eV), N 1 s (401 eV), and O 1 s (538 eV), and their peak shapes are similar, indicating that the constituent elements of their substances are the same. In the high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2 ), C 1s correspond to three peaks, which could be assigned to CC/CC (284.6 eV), CN (285 eV), and CO (286.6 eV) [46]. In Table S1, the proportions of sp2 domains (CC/CC) of the three samples are 25.4 %, 44.8 %, and 65.5 %, respectively, which confirm that the size of sp2 domain increases along with the redshift of photoluminescence wavelength. This result is in good agreement with Raman's results. N 1s could be deconvoluted to 398.6 eV, 399.8 eV, and 401.4 eV ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphical N [50]. The content of graphitic N is another significant parameter responsible for the red shift of photoluminescence. O 1s has two distinct peaks located at 532.1 eV and 533.8 eV attributing to CO and CO, respectively [11]. In Tables S2 and S3, the content of graphitic nitrogen and the oxygen content of the three samples both increased. Hence, the XPS experimental results confirmed that with the red shift of the CDs, the oxidation degree and graphitic nitrogen content of the samples increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible emission and formation mechanism of full-color CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
We investigate the possible formation and emission mechanism of CDs by calculating the band gap difference by cyclic voltammetry. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of colorful CDs were estimated on the base of −4.8 eV (below vacuum level) for FC/FC+. A three - electrode system was used to test the cyclic voltammetric curves, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode for detection and characterization (a modified or bare glassy carbon electrodes) as the working electrode and a Pt wire as the counter electrode. The initial oxidation potential was obtained by using ferrocene as the correction electrode, and the oxidation potential was 0.381 V. The standard initial oxidation potential of panchromatic carbon was measured with a modified electrode. In Fig. 3 , the initial oxidation potential of b-CDs was measured as −0.200 V. Eg is the energy band gap generated by the absorption edge in the absorption peak of CDs (Fig. S5), and they show a decreasing trend. According to empirical formulas (1) and (2), the HOMO and LUMO of CDs could be calculated. The EHOMO and ELUMO of b-CDs can be calculated to be −4.219 and −0.962 eV. In addition, the three-electrode system measures the initial oxidation potential of other carbon dots and calculates the corresponding EHOMO and ELUMO based on the corresponding Eg (Table S4) [51].
(1)
HOMO = -
E OX
- E
F C
/
F
C
+
+ 4.8
(2)
LUMO = HOMO +
E g

The calculated band gap of samples (Table S4) decrease from 3.267 V to 1.486 V, which is consistent with the theory that the reduction of the band gap leads to a gradual redshift of the emission [19].

It is reported that the fluorescence emission spectrum of sp2 carbon tends to yellow and red, and the carboxyl group on sp2 hybrid carbon can induce significant local deformation, resulting in narrower energy gap [20,44]. This discovery is based on density functional theory. Fluorescence is attributed to the surface of CDs, and the surface state controls the photoluminescence characteristics. The band gap between HOMO and LUMO depends largely on the number of merged oxygen atoms, and the band gap decreases with the increase of the number of oxygen atoms. In other words, due to the increase of surface oxidation degree, the photoluminescence red shift is generated.

The hydrated particle size of mp-CDs (Fig. 3a) was significantly higher than that of p-CDs, and the zeta potential of p-CDs and mp-CDs in Fig. 3b were −24.4 eV and 1.25 eV, respectively. The increased electronegativity of mp-CDs explains the successful binding of mannose with p-CDs. In the FT-IR spectrum, the typical carboxylic CO in mp-CDs discretized to other positions compared with p-CDs, and was similar with the peak shape of mannose (Fig. 3c), demonstrating that the mannose is connected to p-CDs. Additionally, the XRD pattern for p-CDs and mp-CDs (Fig. 3d) displays the same peak shape, which indicated they have high crystallinity. In Figs. S2 and S6, the UV absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of p-CDs and mp-CDs were the same, demonstrating that the optical properties of p-CDs were not affected by anchoring mannose.

To evaluate the application of p-CDs and mp-CDs as biomedical fluorescent probes, their cytotoxicity to HepG2 cells was tested using the MTT assay. In Fig. S7, the cell viability after incubation with p-CDs was maintained above 80 %. However, the survival rate of cells decreased gradually with increasing concentration when cells were incubated with mp-CDs, and the cell survival rate was only about 30 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL, indicating that p-CDs modified with mannose had an enhanced cytotoxic effect on cells. Then, we selected two normal cells hepatocytes HL-7702, mouse macrophages RAW264.7, and two cancer cells liver cancer cells HepG2 and cervical cancer Hela cells for the targeting investigation of mp-CDs. In addition, the excitation wavelength of dual channels was selected as 445 nm and 561 nm to avoid possible background fluorescence interference of cells. As displayed in Fig. 4 , only HepG2 cells produced bright fluorescence after incubating with mp-CDs. HepG2 cells showed bright green and red fluorescence in the cell membrane and cytoplasm, but no fluorescence in the nucleus, indicating that mp-CDs could stay in the cell membrane and penetrate the cytoplasm, but could not permeate the nucleus. This is consistent with the fact that mannose receptors are mainly present in HepG2 cells and are delivered to cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis. We then evaluated the effect of culture time on the imaging of mp-CDs in HepG2 cells. A bright fluorescent signal was found at incubation for 30 min (Fig. S8), demonstrating that mp-CDs are extremely easy and rapid to internalize into cells.

We used p-CDs as a control probe for targeting experiments, the only difference between p-CDs and mp-CDs was the presence of mannose modifications. As expected, all cells showed faint fluorescence after incubation with p-CDs (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence intensity of mp-CDs incubated with HepG2 cells at 30 min was used as a reference for quantitative analysis, the fluorescence intensity of p-CDs in the various cells was only about 13 % of the reference value. The above results demonstrated that mp-CDs could rapidly and selectively target HepG2 cells.

The effects of p-CDs and mp-CDs on the apoptosis of HepG2 cells were evaluated by flow cytometry. Mannose allows the rapid receptor-mediated endocytosis of mp-CDs to enter the cells quickly, and act on tumor cells by the combination of mannose and hydroxyurea active functional groups remaining on the surface of carbon dots. Hydroxyurea effectively inhibited the activity of deoxyribonucleotides, DNA synthesis and repair depend heavily on the activity of ribonucleotide reductase, which catalyzes the reduction of ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides using proton-coupled electron transfer reactions. The catalytic reaction of ribonucleotide reductase is a key step in the construction of free nucleic acid libraries, which are substrates needed for DNA replication and repair. In addition, compounds containing hydroxylamine groups (such as hydroxyurea) have the ability to form H2O2 and intermediate free radicals in the organism [52]. It then triggers an uncontrolled free radical chain reaction, which can rapidly disrupt cell metabolism and lead to cell death by altering membrane function through inactivated enzymes, cross-linked DNA, and lipid autoxidation.

Annexin V-APC, a calcium-dependent phospholipid-binding protein, binds to early apoptotic cells via phosphatidylserine exposed on the outside of the cell. 7-AAD could bind specifically to double-stranded DNA and produces intense fluorescence. Under normal conditions, 7-AAD is unable to cross the fine membrane. But at late-stage apoptotic or necrotic cells, it accesses the cell to stain DNA due to the loss of membrane integrity. In the Annexin V-APC (Fig. 6 a) and 7-AAD (Fig. 6b) fluorescence channel, the count of lethal cells caused by mp-CDs was significantly higher than that of p-CDs, suggesting that mp-CDs promote both early and late apoptosis in HepG2 cells. The induction of apoptosis demonstrated that mp-CDs have a potential therapeutic function in cancer cells.

The above targeting assay and flow cytometry showed that mp-CDs not only could rapidly and selectively target HepG2 cells but also induce apoptosis by amplification of hydroxyurea-active functional groups retained on the surface of carbon dots with modified mannose, thus achieving the therapeutic effect of cancer cells.

In conclusion, a series of carbon dots with fluorescence emission from blue to red was synthesized by changing the solvent using the drug hydroxyurea and o-phenylenediamine as the precursor, and the luminescence mechanism was investigated. The matrix to modify mannose in the subsequent cell experiments was chosen to be p-CDs as they had the highest fluorescence quantum yield. It was discovered through a comparison of p-CDs and mp-CDs that mp-CDs displayed a selective targeting effect on HepG2 cells and could enter the cells fast by endocytosis. Mannose can also increase the retention of the hydroxyurea active groups and cause cell death, which may have a therapeutic effect on liver cancer. This simple and feasible strategy may open a way for the combined application of drug-prepared fluorescent probes in biomedicine.

Jiahui Shi: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Juan Ning: Formal analysis. Guizhen Hu: Software, Investigation. Wenzheng Guo: Validation, Funding acquisition. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978069, 22278112), Henan Province Science and Technology Research Project (222102520026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.156564.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
